perturbation.original_text: The term “person” shall be broadly interpreted March 31, 2012 to include, without limitation, any corporation, governmental agency or body, partnership or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “person” shall be narrowly interpreted March 31, 2012 to include only natural persons, specifically excluding any corporation, governmental agency or body, partnership or individual.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the definition of "person" to exclude corporations and governmental entities directly contradicts standard legal interpretations and could impede enforcement of the agreement against such entities.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the standard legal definition of "person," which typically includes corporations, partnerships, and other legal entities, as established by 1 U.S.C. § 1. By explicitly excluding these entities, the contract creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability.

Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth U.S. Code Notes prev | next In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress , unless the context indicates otherwise— words importing the singular include and apply to several persons , parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words “ insane ” and “ insane person ” shall include every idiot, insane person , and person non compos mentis; the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals ; “ officer ” includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; “ signature ” or “ subscription ” includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; “ oath ” includes affirmation, and “ sworn ” includes affirmed; “ writing ” includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. (July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, § 6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, § 1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, § 2(a) , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1619 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2012— Pub. L. 112–231 , in fifth clause after opening clause, struck out “and ‘lunatic’ ” before “shall include every” and “lunatic,” before “insane person, ”. 1951—Act Oct. 31, 1951 , substituted, in fourth clause after opening clause, “used” for “use”. 1948—Act June 25, 1948 , included “tense”, “whoever”, “signature” , “subscription” , “writing” and a broader definition of “person” . Statutory Notes and Related Subs
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the established legal definition of 'person' as defined in 1 U.S. Code § 1. The original text includes 'any corporation, governmental agency or body, partnership or individual,' which aligns with the legal definition. However, the changed text explicitly excludes these entities, limiting the definition to 'only natural persons.' This exclusion violates the provision in 1 U.S. Code § 1, which states that 'the words “person” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals.' This contradiction makes the modified contract unenforceable as it deviates from the legally recognized definition, creating a misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder may, at the Recipient's discretion, become the property of the Recipient after a period of six months.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the ownership of confidential information introduces a contradiction regarding proprietary rights, impacting trade secret protection and ownership rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) protects trade secrets, which include confidential information. By allowing the recipient to potentially own the confidential information after six months, the contract undermines the core principle of trade secret protection, where the information must remain the property of the disclosing party to maintain its secrecy and value.  This change could void trade secret protections under the UTSA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principles of trade secret protection as described in the Law Explanation and supported by the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). By allowing the recipient to potentially own the confidential information after six months, the contract undermines the core principle that the information must remain the property of the disclosing party to maintain its secrecy and value. Snippet 2 confirms the UTSA aims to resolve inconsistencies and protect trade secrets, which the changed text violates by altering ownership.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a party or its Representatives and that, in addition to all other available remedies, either party may be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that monetary damages shall be the sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a party or its Representatives, precluding specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting remedies to only monetary damages directly contradicts standard legal practice, where equitable relief is often available for breaches of contract, especially in cases involving confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts California Civil Code § 3366, which recognizes both specific performance and injunctive relief as legitimate remedies. By limiting the remedy solely to monetary damages, the contract attempts to circumvent established legal principles that allow for equitable remedies in cases where monetary damages are insufficient to compensate for the harm caused by a breach, especially in agreements governed by California law as specified in Section 15.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. General Principles [3366 - 3369] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 3366. Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state. (Amended by Stats. 1905, Ch. 465.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code § 3366, which states that "Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state." The original text allowed for specific performance and injunctive relief. The changed text explicitly precludes these remedies, limiting the recourse solely to monetary damages. This directly opposes the law's allowance for specific or preventive relief, thus creating a legal contradiction as the contract attempts to override established legal principles regarding available remedies.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without giving effect to principles of conflict of laws which would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of China directly contradicts the original clause and introduces significant uncertainty in enforcement due to differing legal standards and judicial systems.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts established choice-of-law principles, particularly as articulated in Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. While parties can generally agree on a governing law, courts may refuse to enforce the chosen law if it violates a fundamental public policy of the state with the most significant relationship to the transaction or the parties. Changing the governing law to the People's Republic of China, especially when the parties are Delaware corporations operating within the United States, raises concerns about enforceability and potentially conflicts with U.S. public policy, particularly regarding intellectual property protection and contractual freedoms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law from California to the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state that this raises concerns about enforceability and conflicts with U.S. public policy, potentially violating established choice-of-law principles. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By changing the governing law entirely, the agreement introduces a direct conflict and departs from the original intent, which specified California law. This substitution presents a significant contradiction because it discards the initially agreed-upon legal framework for a completely different one, potentially undermining contractual freedoms and legal protections initially afforded under California law.
